# Preface {.unnumbered}

These are the lecture notes for *Computational Bioengineering* at TU Wien (2026),
taught by Matthias Függer and Thomas Nowak.

Bioengineering is the systematic engineering of biological entities — designing genetic
circuits, engineering cells for medicine and industry, optimizing bioproduction.
It is an increasingly interdisciplinary field where advances come from biology, computer
science, circuit design, control theory, and more.

In this course you will learn how computational techniques drive progress in bioengineering.
Topics include modeling and simulation of cellular processes, distributed algorithms for
biological systems, design of genetic circuits and digital logic in cells, RNA/DNA
engineering, and computational approaches to measurement.
A concrete running example throughout is the design of an inverter in a bacterial cell.

## Schedule {.unnumbered}

| Session | Date | Topic |
|---------|------|-------|
| 1 | Wed 6/5/2026 | Introduction 1/2 — biological, mathematical, and computational basics |
| 2 | Thu 7/5/2026 | Introduction 2/2 — transcription circuits |
| 3 | Fri 8/5/2026 | Modeling — CRNs, simulation, stochastic simulation |
| 4 | Mon 11/5/2026 | Distributed algorithms |
| 5 | Tue 12/5/2026 | Nucleic acid design |
| 6 | Wed 13/5/2026 | Plasmid design |
| 7 | Mon 18/5/2026 | Digital design |
| 8 | Tue 19/5/2026 | Bulk measurements |
| 9 | Wed 20/5/2026 | Cell measurements |
| 10 | Thu 21/5/2026 | Project kickoff |
| 11 | TBD | Project presentations |

## Examination {.unnumbered}

- Contribution in class: 25 %
- Written report (2 pages): 40 %
- Project presentation: 35 %

## License {.unnumbered}

Course materials are adapted from content by
[Michael Elowitz and Justin Bois](https://biocircuits.github.io/)
and from a previous version of this course at ENS Paris-Saclay
(<https://compbioeng.biodis.co>).
Licensed under [CC BY-NC-SA 4.0](https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-sa/4.0/).
